coredata.dc:title: A novel way for analysis of calycosin via polyaniline functionalized graphene quantum dots fabricated electrochemical sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Jinying
Sun, Bolu
Gou, Xiaodan
Gou, Yuqiang
Li, Wen
Hu, Fangdi
coredata.dc:description: In this study, polyaniline functionalized graphene quantum dots composite with excellent performance was successfully prepared by situ polymerized, and it was applied to fabricate electrochemical sensor for analysis of calycosin in the complex samples of traditional Chinese medicine. The polyaniline functionalized graphene quantum dots composite was characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and Raman analysis, to indicate that polyaniline functionalized graphene quantum dots composite was successfully synthesized. The morphology and microstructure of the modified electrodes were characterized by Scanning electron microscope and Transmission electron microscope. According to the results of cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscope, the glassy carbon electrode modified with polyaniline functionalized graphene quantum dots composite significantly increased the electron transfer rate on the surface of sensor. Under the optimized conditions, the fabricated sensor was used for the analysis of calycosin in Radix Hedysari and Radix Astragali by differential pulse voltammetry, which displayed acceptable reproducibility, long-term stability, good selectivity and sensitivity. The proposed sensor exhibited a wide detection range (1.1 × 10−5–3.52 × 10−4 mol/L) with a detection limit of 9.8 × 10−6 mol/L (S/N = 3). This study provided a novel and convenient method for rapid evaluation of the quality of Radix Hedysari and Radix Astragali.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyaniline functionalized graphene quantum dots (PAGD)
Electrochemical sensor
Calycosin
Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of polyaniline functionalized graphene quantum dots composites (PAGD)
The electrochemical response of calycosin on the PAGD/GCE
Effect of the immobilized amount of PAGD
Determination of calycosin in Radix Astragali and Radix Hedysari

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of the (A) GQDs, (B) PANI, (C) PAGD. TEM images of the (D) GO, (E) GQDs, (F and G) PANI, (H) PAGD.
FT-IR spectra (A) of GO (a), GQDs (b), PANI (c) and PAGD (d). Raman spectra (B) of GO (a), GQDs (b), PANI (c) and PAGD (d).

(A) CV curves of GCE (a and inset), GQDs/GCE (b and inset), PANI/GCE (c) and PAGD/GCE (d) in 5.0 mmol/L [Fe(CN)6)]3−/4− (0.1 mol/L KCl) solution. Scan rate: 100 mV/s. (B) EIS spectra of the GCE (a and inset), GQDs/GCE (b), PANI/GCE (c and inset) and PAGD/GCE (d and inset) in 5.0 mmol/L [Fe(CN)6)]3−/4− (0.1 mol/L KCl). (C) CV curves of GCE (a, b) and PAGD/GCE (c, d) in PBS buffer solution (a, c) and PBS buffer solution containing 28 μmol/L calycosin (b, d). (D) CV curves of GCE in PBS buffer solution (a) and PBS buffer solution containing 28 μmol/L calycosin (b).

(A) the plots of anodic peak currents against the volume of modification. (B) CV curves of calycosin at different pH condition, (C) the relationship between the formal redox potentials and the pH, (D) the relationship between the anodic peak currents and the pH. (E) CV curves of calycosin at different scan rates; (F) the relationship between the logarithm of peak currents and the logarithm of scan rates; (G) the plots of anodic and cathodic potentials against the logarithm of scan rates. (H) the relationship between the anodic peak currents and the accumulation time.

(A) DPV curves of calycosin with different concentrations (a: 11, b: 22, c: 44, d: 88, e: 176, f: 264, g: 352 μmol/L) on PAGD/GCE in pH 3.0 PBS. Conditions: scanned from −0.4 to 0.6 V with amplitude of 0.05 V, and pulse width of 0.05 V. (B) the calibration curve between the anodic peak currents with the concentration of calycosin. (C) Interference of different species to the DPV determination of calycosin with the proposed sensor.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis of PAGD hybrid and the fabrication process of the PAGD/GCE for determination of calycosin.
Comparison of different analytical methods for the detection of calycosin.
Detection of calycosin in Radix Astragali and Radix Hedysari using PAGD/GCE (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A novel way for analysis of calycosin via polyaniline functionalized graphene quantum dots fabricated electrochemical sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, 210046, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Lanzhou Military Command Center for Disease Prevention and Control, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Lanzhou Military Command Center for Disease Prevention and Control

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, polyaniline functionalized graphene quantum dots composite with excellent performance was successfully prepared by situ polymerized, and it was applied to fabricate electrochemical sensor for analysis of calycosin in the complex samples of traditional Chinese medicine. The polyaniline functionalized graphene quantum dots composite was characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and Raman analysis, to indicate that polyaniline functionalized graphene quantum dots composite was successfully synthesized. The morphology and microstructure of the modified electrodes were characterized by Scanning electron microscope and Transmission electron microscope. According to the results of cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscope, the glassy carbon electrode modified with polyaniline functionalized graphene quantum dots composite significantly increased the electron transfer rate on the surface of sensor. Under the optimized conditions, the fabricated sensor was used for the analysis of calycosin in Radix Hedysari and Radix Astragali by differential pulse voltammetry, which displayed acceptable reproducibility, long-term stability, good selectivity and sensitivity. The proposed sensor exhibited a wide detection range (1.1 × 10−5–3.52 × 10−4 mol/L) with a detection limit of 9.8 × 10−6 mol/L (S/N = 3). This study provided a novel and convenient method for rapid evaluation of the quality of Radix Hedysari and Radix Astragali.

ce:section.ce:para: Calycosin, as a typical isoflavonoid, is widely distributed in nature and plays an important role in tumor prevention and treatment [1], inflammation subsides [2], diabetic cognitive impairment [3] and cardiovascular disease [4] due to the extensive pharmacological activities. For instance, it can activate mitogen-activated protein kinase signal transduction pathway after combining with estrogen receptor on cell membrane. Also, calycosin is an index component for the quality tests of Radix Astragali and Radix Hedysari in the Chinese Pharmacopoeia. Therefore, it is worth while establishing a simple, fast, sensitive and accurate method to realize the detection of calycosin in Chinese herbs and preparations, and identify the quality of traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) effectively.

Currently, most of researchers determined the content of calycosin by High performance liquid chromatography [5–7], Liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [8], High performance liquid chromatography-diode array detector-mass spectrometry [9], and so on. These methods have been widely used and generally accepted, but some of them require professionals, complicated pretreatment and expensive instruments, which are not convenient to realize popularization. For these reasons, researchers have paid more and more attention on the good properties of electrochemical analysis [10,11] such as simple sample preparation, easy operation, high sensitivity and fast analysis [12–14]. For instance, Duan et al., [15] constructed a sensor based on Poly (diallyldimethylammonium chloride) functionalized graphene-MoS2 nano-flower materials for the detection of eugenol in TCM, and it showed a good linearity in the range of 0.1 to 440 μmol/L with the lowest detection limit of 0.036 μmol/L (S/N = 3). Sun et al., [16] fabricated an novel sensor based on Fe3O4 magnetic functionalized reduced graphene oxide, which had been used to detect lobetyolin, and the oxidation peak current was proportional to lobetyolin concentration at 1.0 × 10−7 to 1.0 × 10−4 mol/L with a detection limit (S/N = 3) of 4.3 × 10−8 mol/L. It can be seen that electrochemical sensors have unique advantages on sensitive analysis for active ingredients of TCM. Also, they have potential application value on the rapid and convenient evaluation for the quality of TCM.

As is well-known, calycosin, also called 3′, 7-Dihydroxy-4′-methoxyisoflavone, has a highly active isoflavone 3-hydroxyl group [17] in its structure, which is prone to oxidation-reduction reaction. Therefore, the electrochemical signal of the redox reaction occurred on the 3-hydroxyl group can be detected by electrochemical method to analyze the concentration of calycosin in the samples rapidly. Yet until now, there are few reports about the study of electrochemical methods for calycosin. So, it is of great significance to fabricate an electrochemical analysis which is simple, real-time, and efficient for the detection of calycosin in TCM.

With the rapid development of material science, the electrochemical sensors based on new conductive materials have made great progress in improving the intensity and sensitivity of the detection signal. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a kind of graphene layered material with the size <100 nm [18], have attracted enormous attention due to the good electron transfer rate and chemical stability presents, which are caused by the significant quantum confinement and edge effects [19,20]. So, GQDs can be used as an ideal sensor material [21–23]. For example, Razmi et al., [24] constructed an electrochemical sensor based on GQDs for sensitive determination of glucose. The electrochemical response of glucose on the sensor surface was enhanced because of the large specific surface area and the good biocompatibility of GQDs, while the abundant hydrophilic edges and hydrophobic surfaces increased the adsorption of glucose oxidase on the sensor surface. Therefore, GQDs play an important role in increasing the specific surface area of the sensor and improving the analytical performance of electrochemical sensors.

Polyaniline (PANI) is a rising star in conductive polymers, which has attracted great attention because of the special proton doping, high environmental stability, excellent conductivity, high-yield and low cost. It is regarded as one of the most promising conductive polymer materials [25]. PANI nanostructure has been made in the field of electronic device and electrochemical sensors and they have shown good electrochemical properties [26]. While, the composite which is composed of PANI and inorganic conductive nanomaterial possesses the superior characteristics of both materials. So it will become an important way for the development of new conductive materials [27–29]. Sha [30] fabricated an amperometric pH sensor based on graphene-polyaniline. This sensor showed a higher sensitivity than pure Gr-based pH sensor, because graphene-polyaniline composite possessed the characteristics of both the materials and showed good sensitivity. Thus, the composites formed by PANI with graphene or graphene-related carbon materials have unique advantages in increasing the sensitivity of electrochemical sensors.

For the above reasons, we prepared polyaniline functionalized graphene quantum dots (PAGD) by situ polymerized as new sensor material. The –COOH groups of the GQDs could dope PANI well, so they would remain closely adhered to each other and take a good dispersion (Scheme 1 ). Thus, this composite showed a good conductivity. When the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified by PAGD (PAGD/GCE) was applied to the determination of calycosin in complex TCM samples, good electrochemical had been responded. Therefore, the present work offers a new way for the quality certification of TCM.

All electrochemical measurements were implemented on a CHI 6041E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument, China). The pH measurement was performed via pHs-3B digital pH-meter (Shanghai Precision Scientific Instrument, China). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a JEOL JEM-1230 (Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was conducted on a JEOL JSM-6380 LV (Japan). Infrared spectra were obtained on a Nicolet IS5 Infrared spectrometer (Thermo, US); Raman spectra were collected using a Finder One Micro-zone laser Raman microscope (Beijing Zolix Instrument, China). Graphene quantum dots were prepared by a KQ-50 Ultrasonic Cleaner (Kun Shan Ultrasonic Instruments, China).

Calycosin (purity ≥99.0%) and Graphene oxide were supplied by Baoji operation biological technology company (Shanxi, China) and XFNAN technology (Nanjing, China), respectively. Ferric trichloride was purchased from ALFA (Beijing, China). All other chemicals were of analytical grade and all the aqueous solutions were prepared with distilled water.

GQDs were prepared by a simple sono-Fenton method [31]. Firstly, 37.5 mg Graphene oxide (GO) was added into 50 mL distilled water and sonicated for 2 h. Secondly, 15 mL of 30% H2O2 was added along with constant stirring. And then, 7.5 mL FeCl3 (2 mg/mL) was added dropwise with stirring. Finally, the mixture was sonicated in an ultrasonic cleaner for 6 h. The resulting solution was lyophilized to obtain the GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of polyaniline functionalized graphene quantum dots composites (PAGD)
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs with different amounts (5, 10, 20 and 40 mg) was added into 100 μL aniline to prepare PAGD, then the above materials were examined using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As shown in Fig. 3S, when the 10 mg GQDs were added to 100 μL of aniline, the resistance (Ret) is minimum. So, the 10 mg GQDs and 100 μL aniline was selected to prepare PAGD which was used to fabricate the sensor for following experiment. The concrete preparing method is as follows.

Initially, the mixture of 10 mg GQDs and 100 μL aniline was stirred in 10 mL of water at 10–12 °C for 1 h. Then, 5 mL ammonium persulfate (APS, 5% w/v) was added dropwise into mixture with stirring at 5 °C and the solution was left at the same temperature overnight. The product was filtered and washed repeatedly by water and methanol in order to remove APS and oligoaniline. Finally, the residue was naturally dried [32]. The target product was dissolved at 2 mg/mL by distilled water in the follow-up experiment.

The GCE was carefully polished with 0.3 and 0.05 μm Gamma alumina powder on chamois leather, and then ultrasonically washed by water and ethanol several times to remove residual alumina which loosely bound to the electrode surface. This surface was dried by natural drying. Subsequently, 4 μL PAGD (2 mg/mL) was used to preprocess the bare GCE, then the PAGD/GCE electrode was obtained. The prepared PAGD/GCE was washed with water and stored at 4 °C when not in use.

The characterizations of bare GCE, GQDs/GCE, PANI/GCE and PAGD/GCE were investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and EIS in 5.0 mmol/L [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- solution containing 0.10 mol/L KCl. CV was performed by the potential range from −0.2 to 0.5 V at a scan rate of 100 mV/s, The EIS was recorded at potential of 0.2 V and amplitude of 0.005 V with a frequency range from 0. 01 to 105 Hz.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The electrochemical response of calycosin on the PAGD/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The traditional three-electrode system with PAGD/GCE as working electrode, saturated calomel electrode as reference electrode and platinum wire electrode as auxiliary electrode was immersed in 10 mL 0.1 mol/L phosphate buffer saline (PBS), then scanning until the baseline was stable. Subsequently, calycosin standard solution with different concentrations was added into PBS separately, the mixed solution was kept with a homogeneous state. After that, calycosin was enriched on the functionalized sensor by open circuit potential for 180 s, then scanned by CV and recorded. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) in the potential range from −0.4 to 0.6 V. All electrochemical experiment were performed in 0.1 mol/L N2-saturated PBS (pH 3.0) at room temperature, 25 ± 2 °C. In order to maintain reproducibility and stability of the PAGD/GCE, the sensor must be remodeled after each scan.

The Radix Astragali powder (4 g) and methanol (40 mL) was added to a 250 mL round bottom flask, the mixture was extracted by reflux for 1 h at 80 °C, repeating the operating once. Then filtering the extract, the filtrate was combined and concentrated to 10 mL. In addition, Radix Hedysari was treated with the same method.

The surface morphologies of GQDs, PANI and PAGD were examined by SEM. As shown in Fig. 1 , GQDs (Fig. 1A) present a grainy structure which distributed uniformly, PANI (Fig. 1B) is a mixture containing nano-tubular, nano-lamellar and nano-microsphere, while PAGD (Fig. 1C) shows a nano-solid tube shape. Because an end-on lamella is formed and the PANI chains prefer to orient themselves in a longitudinal fashion to produce cylindrical units which further self-assemble to arrange as rods [33].

TEM images of graphene oxide (GO) (Fig. 1D), GQDs (Fig. 1E), PANI (Fig. 1F and Fig. 1G) and PAGD (Fig. 1H) were also obtained. The GO sheet with a wrinkle structure (Fig. 1D) disintegrate into tiny particles (Fig. 1E) due to the sono-Fenton reaction. Such kind of tiny particles are called graphene quantum dot. As shown in Fig. 1E, the diameters of the GQDs are <10 nm. The shapes of PANI are nano-tubular (Fig. 1F) and nano-lamellar (Fig. 1G) and the PAGD (Fig. 1H) shows a nano-solid tube shape. These results were consistent with SEM characterization.

The FTIR was utilized to characterize GO, GQDs, PANI and PAGD (Fig. 2A). From curve b (GQDs), it is found that the GQDs have a broadened peak in the 1600–1750 cm−1 region, which is caused by merging of two peak at 1616 (CC stretching vibrations) and 1735 cm−1 (CO stretching vibrations). Other peaks appear at 1399 cm−1(CH stretching vibration), 1083 cm−1 (CO stretching vibration) and around 3500 cm−1 (OH stretching vibration). The peak at 2300 cm−1 is caused by the influence of atmospheric CO2 [34] and it has nothing to do with the samples (Fig. 2S). Compared with curve a, the characteristic absorption peak of the carboxylic group (1735 cm−1, CO stretching vibrations and 3500 cm−1, OH stretching vibration) and hydroxyl groups (3500 cm−1, OH stretching vibration) in the curve b is larger, indicating that the GQDs (curve b) have more hydrophilic groups than GO (curve a), which indicates that GQDs are more soluble in the aqueous phase compared with GO. These results showed that the preparation of GQDs was successful. As shown on curve c and d, the characteristic absorption bands of PANI appear at 3264 cm−1 (–NH– stretching vibration), 1581 cm−1 (quinonoid (Q) ring deformation), 1499 cm −1 (benzenoid (B) ring deformation), 1300 cm−1 (CN stretching of the secondary aromatic amine), 1143 and 1035 cm−1 (polaron formation of the quinonoid structure (Q = N+ HB or B–N+ HB) due to doping). In the case of the PAGD samples, the presence of all the absorption peaks with a shift from the PANI. Due to the interaction of the carboxyl OH on the GQDs with the secondary amine (–NH–) on the PANI, the OH absorption diminished or even disappeared. In addition, the absorption peak of the quinonoid (Q) ring shifted from 1577 (PANI) to 1582 cm−1 (PAGD), a probable reason is that a rigid quinonoid structure forming upon doping with GQDs [35]. Furthermore, when PANI was doped in GQDs, the π-conjugated system was formed [36], so that the outward bending vibration of the aromatic hydrogen is limited, resulting in γ = C–H moved from 824 to 822 cm−1. The IR spectra show that the PAGD was successfully synthesized.

Raman spectrum is used to detect the ordered or disordered crystal structure of prepared materials. The G-band and the D-band correspond to the ordered Sp2 structure and the disordered degree with the local defect of the edge, respectively. r = ID/IG can be used to measure material defects and chaotic messiness [37]. As shown in Fig. 2B, the rGO (0.51) is less than the rGQDs (0.54), indicating that the order of GQDs weakened. In infrared spectra, these peaks at 1171 (OH bending vibration), 1396 (CN vibration) and 1462 cm−1 (benzene ring CC stretching vibration) of PANI moved to 1178, 1398 and 1560 cm−1 when the polymer was doped in GQDs. The Raman spectra indicated that PAGD had been successfully synthesized, and the results were consistent with the above characterization.

The process for fabrication of PAGD/GCE was characterized by CV and EIS. Fig. 3A displays the CV curves of GCE (a), GQDs/GCE (b), PANI/GCE (c) and PAGD/GCE (d) in 5.0 mmol/L [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- solution (include 0.1 mol/L KCl). The background current of PAGD/GCE is larger than others, illustrating that PAGD enhances the electrochemical response of the PAGD/GCE. The stepwise construction process of the sensor was also characterized by EIS. As shown in Fig. 3B, the PAGD/GCE shows a lower Ret than other modified electrodes, indicating that PAGD increases the electron transfer rate of the surface of proposed sensor.

In this study, NaAc-HAC buffer solution, phosphate buffer saline (PBS) and citrate buffer saline with the concentration of 0.1 mol/L were used to study the effect of buffer solution on the sensor response. Based on the experimental results, it can be concluded that the sensor had a relatively stable baseline and higher sensitivity in PBS. Finally, the PBS was chosen as the supporting electrolyte.

As shown in Fig. 3C, Curve a and c are CV of GCE and PAGD/GCE in PBS, while curve b and d are CV of GCE and PAGD/GCE after the addition of a certain amount of calycosin in PBS. No redox peak was displayed at GCE (a), but a redox peak was showed at PAGD/GCE (c) which was less than curve d (PAGD/GCE in PBS with 28 μmol/L calycosin). Curve c was obtained after multiple CV scans until the curve no longer changed. This phenomenon proved that PAGD had a self-oxidation-reduction reaction which ascribed to the incomplete polymerization of PANI in PAGD, and this reaction did not affect sensitivity of the determination of calycosin by PAGD/GCE (Fig. 1S). After adding 28 μmol/L of calycosin into PBS, the poor redox peaks in GCE was observed (b), while the redox peaks significantly enhanced in PAGD/GCE (d) at Epa = 0.131 V and Epc = 0.382 V (d), ∆Ep = 251 mV, indicating that the calycosin had a good electrochemical response on PAGD/GCE. After repeatedly tests, it proved that this experimental program was feasible and exercisable for analysis of calycosin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the immobilized amount of PAGD
ce:section.ce:para: The immobilized amount of PAGD showed certain influence on electrochemical response of calycosin. According to Fig. 4A, the highest oxidation peak current is achieved at 4 μL, and then the redox peak current decreased with a further raise of PAGD immobilized amount, this was owing to the increased thickness of PAGD film reduced the electron transfer rate. It showed that over immobilized amount resulted in a decrease of sensitivity and the peak current while a lower immobilized amount was not sufficient to effectively enrich the calycosin on the sensor. Therefore, 4 μL of PAGD dispersion was chosen in the follow-up experiment.

CV was used to study the electrochemical behavior of calycosin on PAGD/GCE in different pH in the range from 2.0 to 6.0. As shown in Fig. 4 (B, C, D), the redox peaks positions move negatively with the increasing of pH, which indicating that proton transfer reaction was implied in this process. A good linear relationship between Epa and pH was expressed as Epa = 0.352–0.08 pH, R2 = 0.98 (Epa: V). According to Nernst equation Epa = Eθ − [(2.303mRT)/(nF)] pH [38], and m/n was calculated as 1.35. According to the experiment data, the protons and transferred electrons were identical during the electrode reaction. Moreover, the maximum of oxidation peak current is achieved at pH 3.0. Thus, pH 3.0 was chosen in the follow-up experiments.

CV was used to investigate the electrochemical response of calycosin in the scan rate from 10 to 250 mV/s (Fig. 4E). As shown in Fig. 4F, a linear dependence is established between the logarithm of the anodic peak currents and the logarithm of scan rates, which indicates that the electrode reaction process of calycosin on PAGD/GCE is controlled by adsorption-diffusion mixed. From Fig. 4G, at a high scan rate (the scan rate from 130 to 250 mV/s), the redox peak potential has a linear dependence with the logarithm of scan rates, and the regression equations are Epa = 0.1222 logυ + 0.1472 (Epa: V, υ: V/s), R2 = 0.9932 and Epc = 0.4950 − 0.1622 logυ (Epc: V, υ: V/s), R2 = 0.9815, respectively. According to Laviron's equations [39–40]:
E pa
=
E
θ ′
+
2.303 RT
1 − α
nF
lg v
E pc

E
θ ′

2.303 RT

lg
k s
= α lg
1 − α
+
1 − α
lg α − lg
RT nFv
−
α
1 − α
n F Δ E p
2.3 RT

Therefore, the electron-transfer coefficient (α), electron-transfer number (n), and electrode reaction rate constant (ks) were calculated as 0.44, 0.81 and 1.03/s, respectively, illustrating that calycosin had a rapid electron transfer on PAGD/GCE. In addition, the PAGD provided a larger specific surface area for the proposed sensor, which effectively increased the loading capacity of calycosin on the surface of sensor. Moreover, the excellent conductivity of PAGD promoted electron transfer rate of calycosin on the surface of sensor and enhanced the electrochemical response signal of calycosin. Based on these electrochemical parameters, the reaction mechanism of calycosin on the PAGD/GCE is shown in Scheme 1.

Open circuit potential-time was used to investigate the effect of accumulation time in PBS buffer solution containing 28 μmol/L calycosin. As shown in Fig. 4H, the oxidation peak current increased with the increasing of accumulation time (0, 30, 60, 120, 150, 180, 210, 240, 270 and 300 s), and the oxidation peak current reached the maximum at 180 s. Thus, 180 s was chosen as the optimal enrichment time.

Four sensors modified with PAGD were used to detect PBS buffer solution with 28 μmol/L calycosin. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 2.20%, indicating that the PAGD/GCE has a great reproducibility. The stability of PAGD/GCE was also study by detecting the electrode response in PBS buffer solution with 28 μmol/L calycosin at an interval of 7 and 14 days. The sensors were stored at 4 °C after each experiment. The current response decreased to the 98.6% and 97.1% of the original response respectively, after the sensors had been stored 7 and 14 days.

Good selectivity is crucial for electrochemical sensors to study the electroactive constituents of TCM. In this study, various inorganic ions and molecules were studied on the basis of their interferences in the determination of calycosin. The interferences effects of quercetin, formononetin, glycine, glutamic acid, L-rhamnose, sucrose, Calycosin-7-O-β-D-glucopyranoside and some inorganic ions like Fe3+, Cu2+, Mg2+ and Ca2+ with high concentrations were examined (Table 1S). In this study, 10 mL of PBS containing 28 μmol/L calycosin and different concentration of possible interference were prepared to record the DPV. As shown in Fig. 5C, the recovery of calycosin varied from 91.22% to 105.85%, indicating that the PAGD/GCE was not significantly affected by the interferences with high concentration in the analysis of calycosin. Therefore, the proposed sensor has a good selectivity for the detection of calycosin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of calycosin in Radix Astragali and Radix Hedysari
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of calycosin in Radix Astragali and Radix Hedysari was determined by PAGD/GCE. Transferred 100 μL extracts of Radix Astragali or Radix Hedysari to 25 mL beaker, diluted with PBS to 10 mL, and mixed. The solution was prepared within the linear limitation, and calycosin was detected by standard addition method. The concentration of Radix Astragali and Radix Hedysari extracts was also detected by HPLC, and the data of HPLC were regarded as the true value (Table 2S). As shown in Table 2 , the recovery for the determination of calycosin was in the range of 98.8% to 102.4%, indicating that the PAGD/GCE could be used for the sensitive determination of calycosin in real samples.

In this study, Polyaniline functionalized graphene quantum dots composite (PAGD) with excellent performance was successfully prepared by situ polymerized, and it was applied to fabricate a novel electrochemical sensor for the analysis of calycosin in the complex samples of TCM. The electrochemical behavior of calycosin on the proposed sensor was investigated by CV and EIS, suggesting that PAGD with large specific surface area provided a large number of active sites for the calycosin, and it also greatly enhanced the electrochemical response of calycosin on the sensor. Under the optimal conditions, PAGD/GCE sensors had a wide linear range and low detection limit for calycosin, and it exhibited reasonable stability, selectivity and good reproducibility. Moreover, the acceptable recovery was obtained when the sensor was applied to the analysis of calycosin in the extracts of Radix Astragali and Radix Hedysari. In a word, the research provided a novel method for the rapid evaluation of the quality of Radix Astragali and Radix Hedysari and also deepened the application of PAGD in the field of drug analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Science and Technology support Project in Gansu Province (1504FKCA010), the Planned Project of the Gansu Province Intellectual Property Office (17ZC1LA016) and Science and Technology support Project of Gansu Province (2016-2-80).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2018.03.035.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antineoplastic effect of calycosin on osteosarcoma through inducing apoptosis showing in vitro and in vivo investigations
Exp. Mol. Pathol.
